1:
On Numbers 7:1 — “And it came to pass on the day that Moses had fully set up the tabernacle, and had anointed it, and sanctified it… and the altar and all the vessels thereof” — the witnesses mark the offering of the princes when the tabernacle was finished.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: when the tabernacle was fully set up, the princes prepared six covered wagons and twelve oxen for its service. The Puritan Henry: the offering came not till all was fully set up, both the tabernacle and the camp about it. The Reformed Calvin: this was a second contribution of the people, after the completion of the tabernacle. Wesley: “on the day” is for “about the time,” for the princes offered on several days.

The verse sets the occasion. When the tabernacle was fully set up, anointed, and sanctified, the princes offered. The dedication-gifts follow the finishing of the sanctuary.

So the witnesses see the princes’ offering brought when the tabernacle was at last fully reared, anointed, and hallowed. The witnesses note the timing: “on the day that Moses had fully set up the tabernacle” — Henry observes the offering came not until all was finished, the dwelling itself and the camp ordered about it, so that the gifts crowned a completed work. They mark Wesley’s point that “the day” here stands for the time or season, since (the chapter shows) the princes did not all offer on one day but on successive days; the word is not to be pressed to a single date. They observe the anointing and sanctifying: the tabernacle, the altar, and all the vessels had been anointed with the holy oil and set apart for God (Exodus 40:9; Leviticus 8:10); the sanctuary was consecrated before its service began. They note Calvin’s observation that this was a second free contribution of the people, after the great giving for the building of the tabernacle (Exodus 35:21); their devotion did not cease with the raising of the structure, but went on to furnish its service. They observe the readiness of the princes the witnesses commend: the leaders of the tribes led also in giving, offering at their own charge for the worship of God. They note that the finished sanctuary called forth fresh liberality. The verse thus sets the occasion: the tabernacle fully set up, the princes offering — the gifts named in the next verses.
2:
On Numbers 7:2 — “That the princes of Israel, heads of the house of their fathers… the princes of the tribes… offered” — the witnesses mark the princes as the offerers.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the princes of Israel, heads of their fathers’ houses, who were over them that were numbered, presented the six wagons and twelve oxen. The Reformed Gill: the princes of the twelve tribes. Wesley: they offered in the manner and on the days hereafter mentioned.

The verse names the offerers. The princes of the tribes, the heads of the fathers’ houses, brought the offering. The leaders give first.

So the witnesses see the offering brought by the princes, the heads and leaders of the tribes of Israel. The witnesses note who they were: “the princes of Israel, heads of the house of their fathers… the princes of the tribes” — Keil and Delitzsch and Gill identify them as the twelve tribal princes, the same men who had been set over the numbering of the people (ch. 1–2) and made captains over the hosts; the chief men of each tribe. They mark that these leaders, who held the foremost place in rule, took also the foremost place in giving; the princes led their tribes in liberality to the house of God as they led them in the camp and the march. They observe the representative character the witnesses note: though the princes are named as the offerers, the gifts were in truth from the whole tribe, the prince presenting (as Calvin observed) what his tribe had subscribed; so the leaders gave on behalf of all. They note Wesley’s observation that they offered in the order and on the days afterward set down, each in his turn. They observe the fitness of leaders setting the example in devotion: when those in high place give freely and gladly to God, the people are stirred to follow. They note that the princes, having helped to number and order the host, now led it in worship. They observe that rule and generosity went together in them. The verse thus names the offerers: the princes of the tribes — their gift specified in the next verse.
3:
On Numbers 7:3 — “And they brought their offering before the LORD, six covered wagons, and twelve oxen; a wagon for two of the princes, and for each one an ox” — the witnesses mark the wagons and oxen for the tabernacle’s service.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: six covered wagons and twelve oxen, a wagon for two princes and an ox for each. Adam Clarke: “covered” or tilted wagons, the rendering uncertain. The Reformed Calvin: dedicated for the conveyance of the tabernacle, whose pillars and parts could not be borne on men’s shoulders. Wesley: for the more convenient and safe carriage of the cumbersome things.

The verse names the gift. Six covered wagons and twelve oxen, brought before the LORD for carrying the tabernacle. The princes furnish the means of transport.

So the witnesses see the princes’ first gift: wagons and oxen for the carriage of the tabernacle on the march. The witnesses note the offering: “six covered wagons, and twelve oxen; a wagon for two of the princes, and for each one an ox” — the twelve princes together provided six covered (or tilted) wagons and twelve draught oxen, each pair of princes joining for a wagon and each prince giving an ox. They mark the uncertainty of the word “covered” that Clarke notes: the Hebrew is variously rendered (tilted, awninged), the ancient versions guessing at its sense; the wagons were at least roofed or covered, fit to protect what they bore. They observe the purpose Calvin and Wesley give: the wagons were dedicated for the conveyance of the tabernacle, whose pillars, boards, and heavy parts could not be carried on men’s shoulders, but needed wheels for their safe and convenient transport through the wilderness. They note the wisdom and care of the princes’ gift: they provided not gold or show, but the practical means by which the holy dwelling could be moved as Israel journeyed; their liberality served the real need of God’s service. They observe that the offering was brought “before the LORD,” before the tabernacle where he now dwelt, presented to him as a freewill gift. They note that devotion shows itself in furnishing what the worship of God requires. The verse thus names the gift: six wagons and twelve oxen — which God bids Moses receive in the next verses.
4:
On Numbers 7:4 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark God’s word approving the gift.

The Reformed Gill: the LORD spake unto Moses out of the tabernacle, before which the wagons and oxen were brought.

The verse opens God’s answer. The LORD speaks to Moses concerning the offering. The gift is met with a divine word.

So the witnesses see God himself speak to direct the receiving and use of the princes’ gift. The witnesses note the source of the word: Gill observes that the LORD spake unto Moses out of the tabernacle, before which the wagons and oxen had been brought; God answered from his dwelling, the place of his manifested presence, where he had promised to commune with Moses (Exodus 25:22). They mark the significance of God’s speaking at all: the freewill offering of the princes was not left to Moses’ own judgment, but God himself gave direction concerning it, showing that he regarded and accepted the gift, and would order its use to his service. They observe that the divine word here both approved the offering and instructed how it should be applied: the wagons and oxen were to be received and given to the Levites for the carriage of the tabernacle, each according to his need. They note the condescension of God, who takes notice of his people’s gifts and speaks concerning them; nothing offered to him in faith is overlooked, but he receives and disposes it. They observe that Moses, the faithful steward, did nothing of his own in the matter, but waited on God’s word and obeyed it. They note that the gift was honoured by being made the subject of a divine command. They observe that God’s acceptance crowned the princes’ liberality. The verse thus opens God’s answer: the LORD speaks concerning the gift — the command to receive it following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in this brief word, the dignity that God’s notice put upon the princes’ gift. They observe that the freewill offering was not left to Moses’ own discretion, but became the subject of an express divine command, God himself speaking from the tabernacle to direct its receiving and its use. They note the condescension of God, who stoops to take notice even of wagons and oxen offered in faith, and who orders their distribution to his service; nothing brought to him in love is overlooked or despised. They mark that Moses, the faithful steward, did nothing of his own in the matter, but waited on the word of the LORD and obeyed it, as careful in the small thing as in the great. They observe that the gift, honoured by a divine command, was thereby owned and accepted, the princes’ liberality crowned by God’s approval.
5:
On Numbers 7:5 — “Take it of them, that they may be to do the service of the tabernacle of the congregation; and thou shalt give them unto the Levites, to every man according to his service” — the witnesses mark the gift accepted and assigned to the Levites.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: take it, that they may serve the tabernacle, and give them to the Levites according to each man’s service. Adam Clarke and Wesley: distribute them as needed, most to those with the heaviest burdens. The Reformed Gill: by this the freewill offering was according to God’s mind, well pleasing to him.

The verse accepts the gift. God bids Moses take it for the tabernacle’s service and give it to the Levites by their need. The offering is received and put to use.

So the witnesses see God accept the princes’ gift and appoint its use for the Levites who served the tabernacle. The witnesses note the command to receive: “take it of them” — Gill observes that by this God showed the freewill offering of the princes was according to his mind and will, well pleasing in his sight, and indeed prompted by his own Spirit; God does not refuse what is offered him in faith, but takes it. They mark the use appointed: “that they may be to do the service of the tabernacle… thou shalt give them unto the Levites” — the wagons and oxen were to be put to the carriage of the tabernacle and given over to the Levites, who bore that charge. They observe the rule of distribution: “to every man according to his service” — Clarke and Wesley draw out that the wagons were to be shared among the Levites as their burdens required, most given to those who had the heaviest things to carry, fewer to those with less; the gift was apportioned to the need, not divided equally without regard to the task. They note the wisdom and order in this: God’s service was furnished, and each company of Levites supplied according to its work, neither overburdened nor left wanting. They observe that the princes’ liberality, received and rightly distributed, lightened the Levites’ labour. They note that what is given to God is used for his service and his servants. The verse thus accepts the gift and assigns it: to the Levites by their need — Moses doing so in the next verses.
6:
On Numbers 7:6 — “And Moses took the wagons and the oxen, and gave them unto the Levites” — the witnesses mark Moses’ obedient distribution.

The Reformed Gill: Moses took the wagons and the oxen of the princes, as he was directed, and gave them to the Levites.

The verse records the act. Moses takes the wagons and oxen and gives them to the Levites. The command is at once obeyed.

So the witnesses see Moses promptly carry out God’s direction, receiving the gift and handing it to the Levites. The witnesses note the obedience Gill marks: “Moses took the wagons and the oxen… and gave them unto the Levites” — exactly as he had been directed (v. 5), Moses received the princes’ offering and distributed it to the Levites for the service of the tabernacle; the steward did just as his Lord commanded, neither adding nor withholding. They mark the faithfulness of Moses in this small matter as in great ones: he waited for God’s word concerning the gift, and then performed it without delay; the man of God is as careful to obey in the ordering of wagons as in the weightier things of the law. They observe the fitness of the arrangement now begun: the gift of the princes passed, by God’s appointment and Moses’ hand, to the Levites who needed it for their burdens; the liberality of the leaders became the help of the ministers of the sanctuary. They note that God’s house is served when each does his part — the princes giving, Moses distributing, the Levites bearing. They observe that the offering, received and rightly placed, was now ready to be apportioned among the Levite families according to their several charges. They note that obedience completed the receiving of the gift. The verse thus records the act: Moses gives the wagons to the Levites — the particular division following in the next verses.

So the witnesses see in Moses’ prompt obedience the faithfulness of a true steward in God’s house. They observe that, having received the divine direction, he at once took the wagons and oxen and gave them to the Levites, neither adding to the command nor withholding from it; the man of God was as careful to obey in the ordering of carts as in the weightier matters of the law. They note that the gift thus passed, by God’s appointment and Moses’ hand, from the princes who gave to the Levites who needed it, the liberality of the leaders becoming the help of the ministers of the sanctuary. They mark that God’s house is served when each does his part — the princes giving, Moses distributing, the Levites bearing. They observe that obedience completed the receiving of the gift, and that the offering was now ready to be apportioned according to each family’s charge.
7:
On Numbers 7:7 — “Two wagons and four oxen he gave unto the sons of Gershon, according unto their service” — the witnesses mark the portion of the Gershonites.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the Gershonites carried the curtains, coverings, and hangings (Numbers 4:25); a cumbersome but not heavy load, so they needed but two wagons. The Reformed Gill: two wagons and four oxen to the sons of Gershon, the eldest son of Levi.

The verse gives Gershon two wagons. The sons of Gershon receive two wagons and four oxen for their burden. The lighter load gets the smaller share.

So the witnesses see the first portion of the wagons assigned to the Gershonites, according to the rule of need. The witnesses note the allotment: “two wagons and four oxen he gave unto the sons of Gershon” — Gill identifies them as the family of Gershon, the eldest son of Levi; to them were given two of the six wagons and four of the twelve oxen. They mark the reason Clarke draws from their charge: the Gershonites had the carrying of the curtains, coverings, and hangings of the tabernacle (Numbers 4:25); these were bulky and cumbersome, taking up room, yet not of great weight, so that two wagons sufficed for their load. They observe the wisdom of the distribution “according unto their service”: the wagons were apportioned to the nature of each family’s burden, the Gershonites’ load being large in bulk but light in weight, fitly carried in two wagons. They note that God’s ordering matched the help to the task, neither giving more than was needed nor less; the service was furnished with exactness. They observe that the curtains and hangings, the soft furnishings of the holy place, were thus provided for in their carriage. They note that every part of the tabernacle, down to its coverings, had its appointed bearers and means. They observe that the gift of the princes served the real needs of the sanctuary’s removal. The verse thus gives Gershon two wagons: for their lighter, bulkier load — Merari’s larger portion following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in the apportioning to Gershon the wisdom of a distribution measured to the need. They observe that the wagons were given “according unto their service,” the Gershonites’ load of curtains and hangings being large in bulk yet light in weight, so that two wagons sufficed; God matched the help to the burden, neither more nor less than the task required. They note that every part of the tabernacle, down to its coverings and curtains, had its appointed bearers and its means of carriage, that nothing be left unprovided for in the holy dwelling’s removal. They mark that the gift of the princes thus served the real and particular needs of God’s service, the liberality of the leaders lightening the labour of the ministers. They observe that God’s ordering of his house is exact and equitable, supplying each according to his work.
8:
On Numbers 7:8 — “And four wagons and eight oxen he gave unto the sons of Merari, according unto their service, under the hand of Ithamar the son of Aaron the priest” — the witnesses mark the larger portion of the Merarites.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: four wagons and eight oxen to the sons of Merari, under Ithamar. Adam Clarke: because they bore the boards, bars, pillars, and sockets (Numbers 4:31), they had twice as many wagons as the Gershonites. The Reformed Gill: all the remaining wagons and oxen, double the Gershonites’ number.

The verse gives Merari four wagons. The sons of Merari receive four wagons and eight oxen for their heavy load. The heaviest burden gets the largest share.

So the witnesses see the larger portion of the wagons assigned to the Merarites, whose burden was the heaviest. The witnesses note the allotment: “four wagons and eight oxen he gave unto the sons of Merari” — Gill observes these were all the remaining wagons and oxen, double the number given to the Gershonites. They mark the reason Clarke draws from their charge: the Merarites had to carry the boards, bars, pillars, sockets, and pins of the tabernacle (Numbers 4:31–32), the heavy timber and metal framework; so weighty a load required twice the wagons of the Gershonites’ lighter burden. They observe the rule “according unto their service” once more at work: the help was apportioned to the weight of the task, the heaviest-laden family given the most wagons and oxen; God’s distribution was exact and equitable, measured to each by his burden. They note the oversight named: “under the hand of Ithamar the son of Aaron the priest” — the service of both Gershon and Merari was under the charge of Ithamar, a son of Aaron, that the work might be ordered and overseen by the priesthood. They observe that God so furnished his service that the heaviest burdens were the best supplied; none was left to bear beyond his help. They note that the frame of the holy dwelling was provided for in its carriage. The verse thus gives Merari four wagons: for their heavy load, under Ithamar — while Kohath, the next verse shows, received none.
9:
On Numbers 7:9 — “But unto the sons of Kohath he gave none: because the service of the sanctuary belonging unto them was that they should bear upon their shoulders” — the witnesses mark the Kohathites bearing the holy things on their shoulders.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the Kohathites had the charge of the ark, table, candlestick, and altars (Numbers 4:15), to be carried on their shoulders; those sacred things must not be drawn by beasts. The Reformed Gill: none given them, all being disposed to Gershon and Merari. Wesley: borne on the shoulders, because of the greater worth and holiness of the things they carried.

The verse gives Kohath none. They received no wagons, for they bore the holy things on their shoulders. The most sacred load was carried by hand.

So the witnesses see the Kohathites given no wagons, because their sacred burden was to be borne upon their shoulders, not drawn by oxen. The witnesses note the exception: “unto the sons of Kohath he gave none” — Gill observes that all the wagons were disposed to Gershon and Merari, and none to Kohath; not from neglect, but for the reason the verse gives. They mark the charge of the Kohathites that Clarke records: they had the carrying of the most holy things — the ark, the table of shewbread, the candlestick, and the altars (Numbers 4:15); and these, by God’s appointment, were to be carried on the shoulders of the Levites, borne by hand, not loaded upon wagons. They observe the reason Wesley gives: because of the greater worth and holiness of the things they carried, the sacred vessels were not to be drawn by beasts like common freight, but borne reverently upon men’s shoulders, as a token of their sanctity. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: the holiest things demanded the most reverent handling; what was nearest to God was carried most carefully, by consecrated men, not by ox and cart. They recall the later warning of this very rule, when the ark, set on a new cart contrary to the command, brought death (2 Samuel 6:6); the holy things must be borne as God appointed. They observe that reverence, not convenience, governed the carriage of the sacred. The verse thus gives Kohath none: they bear the holy things on their shoulders — the dedication of the altar following in the next verses.
10:
On Numbers 7:10 — “And the princes offered for dedicating of the altar in the day that it was anointed, even the princes offered their offering before the altar” — the witnesses mark the dedication of the altar.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: every prince offered on behalf, and at the expense, of his whole tribe. The Puritan Henry: the dedicating of the altars, which had been sanctified before by anointing (Leviticus 8:10), now handselled by use. The Reformed Calvin: another kind of offering, a silver dish and bowl and a golden spoon from each tribe. Wesley: not the first consecration, but a festal dedication by use.

The verse opens the altar’s dedication. The princes offer before the altar in the day it was anointed. The altar is dedicated by their gifts.

So the witnesses see the princes turn from the gift of wagons to the dedication of the altar with their offerings. The witnesses note the occasion: “the princes offered for dedicating of the altar in the day that it was anointed” — the altar, having been anointed and sanctified with the holy oil (as Henry notes from Leviticus 8:10), was now dedicated by the princes’ gifts and the first solemn offerings upon it. They mark the distinction the witnesses keep: this was not the first consecration of the altar (that had been done by Moses and Aaron with the anointing oil), but, as Wesley and Henry put it, a festal dedication by use, a “handselling” of the altar, the bringing of it into joyful service with thanksgiving. They observe the representative giving Clarke notes: each prince offered before the altar on behalf of, and at the charge of, his whole tribe; the gift was the tribe’s, presented by its head. They observe the kinds of gift Calvin previews: a silver charger and bowl and a golden spoon from each tribe, besides the sacrifices, to be named in the verses following. They note the fitness of dedicating the altar with both costly vessels and full sacrifice: the place where God met his people in offering was hallowed by the people’s gladdest devotion. They observe that the altar, the centre of Israel’s worship, was honoured at its first use with the leaders’ liberality. They note that the dedication was a great solemnity of joy. The verse thus opens the altar’s dedication: the princes offering before it — the order of their offering given in the next verse.
11:
On Numbers 7:11 — “And the LORD said unto Moses, They shall offer their offering, each prince on his day, for the dedicating of the altar” — the witnesses mark the order, one prince each day.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: each prince on his day, for the dedicating of the altar; the first day Nahshon of Judah. The Reformed Gill: Moses knew not in what order they should offer — by birth or by the journeying — till God directed. Wesley: in this offering they followed the order of their camp, not of their birth.

The verse fixes the order. By God’s word, each prince offers on his own day. The twelve give in turn, day by day.

So the witnesses see God appoint that the princes offer not all at once but one each day, in a fixed order. The witnesses note the divine direction: “they shall offer their offering, each prince on his day” — Gill observes that Moses himself knew not in what order they should offer, whether by the order of their birth or by some other rule, until God directed him; the matter was settled by express divine word. They mark the order chosen, which Wesley and Keil note: the princes offered in the order of their camp and march — Judah first, then the rest as they were ranged about the tabernacle — not by the order of their birth; so Nahshon of Judah, whose standard led the host, offered on the first day. They observe the reason the witnesses see in the day-by-day arrangement: that each tribe might have its own day of honour before the LORD, the joy of the dedication prolonged through twelve days, and each prince’s offering received with full solemnity, the priests attending each in turn. They note that God thus prevented all rivalry and confusion: none offered before his appointed day, none was slighted; the order was God’s, and every tribe had its equal place. They observe that the spreading of the offerings over twelve days made the dedication a sustained feast of devotion. They note that God orders even the times of his people’s giving. The verse thus fixes the order: each prince on his day — beginning, in the next verse, with Judah.
12:
On Numbers 7:12 — “And he that offered his offering the first day was Nahshon the son of Amminadab, of the tribe of Judah” — the witnesses mark Judah offering first.

The Reformed Gill: he whose standard was pitched first, at the east toward the rising sun, offered first. Calvin: the oracle of Jacob is well known — “The scepter shall not depart from Judah” (Genesis 49:10); though the time of its fulfilment was not yet come, Judah held the first place.

The verse names the first offerer. Nahshon, prince of Judah, offers on the first day. The royal tribe leads in the dedication as in the camp.

So the witnesses see Judah, through its prince Nahshon, lead the twelve days of offering, as it led the camp and the march. The witnesses note the precedence: “he that offered… the first day was Nahshon the son of Amminadab, of the tribe of Judah” — Gill observes that he whose standard was pitched first, on the east toward the rising sun, offered first; the order of the camp determined the order of the dedication, and Judah stood at the head. They mark the deeper ground Calvin draws out: the well-known oracle of dying Jacob, “The scepter shall not depart from Judah” (Genesis 49:10); though the time of that prophecy’s open fulfilment in the kingdom and in Christ was not yet come, yet Judah already held the first place among the tribes, a foreshadowing of the royal dignity that would centre in it. They observe the man named: Nahshon the son of Amminadab, prince of Judah, who stands in the genealogy of David and of the Messiah (Matthew 1:4); the offering of the dedication was led by an ancestor of Christ. They note the typology the witnesses see: the tribe from which the King and Saviour would come went first in the worship of God, as in the order of the host; the line of promise led the people in devotion. They observe that God’s ordering honoured Judah in every place — in camp, march, and offering. The verse thus names the first offerer: Nahshon of Judah — his offering detailed in the next verses.
13:
On Numbers 7:13 — “And his offering was one silver charger… one silver bowl of seventy shekels… both of them full of fine flour mingled with oil for a meat offering” — the witnesses mark the silver vessels and the meat offering.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: a silver charger of a hundred and thirty shekels, a silver bowl of seventy, both full of fine flour with oil for a meat offering. Adam Clarke: the charger a deep dish for kneading paste; the bowl a basin to receive the blood (Exodus 27:3). The Reformed Gill and Wesley: the silver vessels for the altar and court, not the sanctuary, whose vessels were of gold.

The verse names the silver vessels. A silver charger and bowl, both full of fine flour with oil, for a meat offering. The first prince gives plate and meal.

So the witnesses see the substance of each prince’s gift, here first set out in full for Judah: silver vessels and a meat offering. The witnesses note the two pieces of plate: “one silver charger… one silver bowl” — Clarke explains the charger as a deep dish (such as was used for kneading the paste of the meal offering) and the bowl as a basin to receive the blood of the sacrifices (Exodus 27:3); both of silver, and (as Gill and Wesley note) belonging to the service of the altar and court, not the inner sanctuary, whose vessels were of gold. They mark the weights, after the shekel of the sanctuary: the charger a hundred and thirty shekels, the bowl seventy, costly vessels of solid silver. They observe the contents: “both of them full of fine flour mingled with oil for a meat offering” — the vessels were not empty but filled with the meal offering, fine flour and oil, the offering of the fruits of the ground, expressing thankful dedication of God’s gifts and (with the oil) the grace that sanctifies. They note the fitness of the meat offering at the dedication: the bread of life offered to God, a token that all the people’s sustenance was his and from him. They observe the typology the witnesses suggest throughout: the fine flour without leaven points to Christ, the bread of life, perfect and pure, mingled with the oil of the Spirit. They note that the gift was both costly and significant. The verse thus names the silver vessels and meat offering — the golden spoon of incense following in the next verse.
14:
On Numbers 7:14 — “One spoon of ten shekels of gold, full of incense” — the witnesses mark the golden spoon of incense.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the “spoon” (caph) a censer on which they put the incense (Exodus 25:29); the tribes brought their offerings in the order of their encampment. The Reformed Gill: of gold, weighing ten shekels, about four ounces.

The verse names the gold spoon. A golden spoon of ten shekels, full of incense. The first prince gives gold and incense.

So the witnesses see the third item of each prince’s gift: a golden spoon or censer, full of incense. The witnesses note the vessel: “one spoon of ten shekels of gold” — Clarke explains the Hebrew caph as a small censer or pan on which the incense was put and burned (Exodus 25:29); Gill reckons its weight at ten shekels, about four ounces of gold. They mark that this vessel, unlike the silver charger and bowl, was of gold; for it pertained to the incense, which was burned before the LORD, and the things nearest the holy service were of the more precious metal. They observe the contents: “full of incense” — the spoon was filled with the holy incense, the sweet spices burned to God. They note the meaning the witnesses draw from incense throughout Scripture: it is the figure of prayer, the prayers of the saints ascending to God as a sweet savour (Psalm 141:2; Revelation 8:3), and, in the fuller light, of the intercession of Christ, whose merit makes the people’s prayers acceptable. They observe Clarke’s further note that the tribes brought their offerings precisely in the order in which they encamped about the tabernacle, the dedication following the arrangement of the camp. They note the fitness of incense at the dedication: with the gift of vessels and food was joined the offering of prayer and praise. They observe that costly gold fitly held the emblem of prayer. The verse thus names the gold spoon of incense — the burnt offering following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in this golden spoon of incense, brought by every prince alike, the offering of prayer joined to the gifts of substance and sacrifice. They observe that the costliest metal, gold, fitly bore the emblem of prayer, for the things nearest the holy service of God were of gold; and that the sweet incense, burned before the LORD, figured the prayers of the saints ascending as a sweet savour (Revelation 8:3), made acceptable through the intercession of Christ. They note that every tribe brought the same spoon and the same incense, that the prayer and praise of all might rise alike to God, none preferred and none despised in this sweet service. They mark that Scripture records each spoon in full, writing out the devotion of every tribe by name, so that the prayers of all the people stand on record before God, who forgets none of his own.
15:
On Numbers 7:15 — “One young bullock, one ram, one lamb of the first year, for a burnt offering” — the witnesses mark the burnt offering.

The Reformed Gill: one young bullock, of three years old, as the Targums say, with a ram and a lamb, for a burnt offering.

The verse names the burnt offering. A bullock, a ram, and a lamb are offered whole to God. Entire consecration is given.

So the witnesses see the prince’s sacrifice begin with a burnt offering of three beasts wholly given to God. The witnesses note the victims: “one young bullock, one ram, one lamb of the first year” — Gill observes (with the Targums) that the bullock was of three years old, and these three — bullock, ram, and lamb — were brought together for a burnt offering. They mark the nature of the burnt offering: it was wholly consumed upon the altar, ascending entire to God in flame and smoke, the offerer keeping no part; it expressed total consecration and self-dedication, the giving up of all to the LORD. They observe the fitness of a burnt offering at the dedication of the altar: the first great act of worship upon the newly hallowed altar was the entire surrender of the best of the herd and flock to God, a token that the people and all they had were wholly his. They note the typology the witnesses carry through the chapter: the unblemished victims, wholly offered, point to Christ, who offered himself without spot to God (Hebrews 9:14), the perfect burnt offering of entire devotion. They observe that each prince brought the same burnt offering, none more and none less, so that every tribe was alike consecrated to God at the altar. They note that the dedication was sealed with whole burnt sacrifice, the costliest token of devotion. They observe that consecration came before communion in the order of the offerings. The verse thus names the burnt offering: bullock, ram, and lamb — the sin offering following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in this burnt offering, brought by every prince alike, the entire consecration of each tribe to God. They observe that the bullock, ram, and lamb were wholly consumed upon the altar, ascending in flame and smoke to the LORD, the offerer keeping no part; the whole burnt offering was the costliest token of devotion, the giving up of all to God. They note that each tribe brought the same three victims, neither more nor less, so that all were alike consecrated to him who dwelt in the midst, and no tribe could boast of a greater surrender or be charged with a smaller; in the worship of God there is no room for rivalry. They mark the typology that runs through the chapter: the unblemished victims, wholly offered, point to Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God (Hebrews 9:14), the perfect burnt offering of entire devotion.
16:
On Numbers 7:16 — “One kid of the goats for a sin offering” — the witnesses mark the sin offering joined to the joyful dedication.

The Reformed Gill: though these offerings were by way of thanksgiving, to express joy at the erection of the tabernacle and altar, yet a sin offering was added, since even in their best services sin might not be wanting.

The verse names the sin offering. A kid of the goats is offered for sin. Atonement is joined even to the dedication’s joy.

So the witnesses see a sin offering set in the midst of the joyful dedication gifts, that atonement might not be wanting even in thanksgiving. The witnesses note the victim: “one kid of the goats for a sin offering” — a young goat brought as a sin offering, alongside the burnt offering and the peace offerings. They mark the striking observation Gill draws out: though these offerings of the princes were by way of thanksgiving, to express their joy and gladness at the erection of the tabernacle, its altars, and the service thereof, yet a sin offering was joined to them; for even in their best and most joyful services there might be, and was, some sin and imperfection, which needed atonement. They observe the lesson the witnesses press: that no act of worship, however glad or costly, is so pure that it does not need the cleansing of an atoning sacrifice; the holiest dedication must be accompanied by a sin offering, lest the very offering be defiled by the offerer’s sin. They note the humbling truth in this: man cannot bring even a thank offering to God without the taint of sin, and so must ever come by way of atonement; the kid of the sin offering owned the imperfection of all they did. They observe the typology the witnesses see: the sin offering points to Christ, made sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21), through whose atonement alone our best services are accepted. They note that joy and the sense of sin were rightly held together. The verse thus names the sin offering: a kid, for atonement in the dedication — the peace offerings following in the next verse.
17:
On Numbers 7:17 — “And for a sacrifice of peace offerings, two oxen, five rams, five he goats, five lambs of the first year: this was the offering of Nahshon the son of Amminadab” — the witnesses mark the peace offerings completing Judah’s gift.

The Reformed Gill: here were all sorts of offerings — meat and drink, burnt, sin, and peace offerings. Wesley: the peace offerings more numerous, because the princes, priests, and people made a feast before the Lord out of them.

The verse names the peace offerings. Two oxen, five rams, five goats, five lambs complete Judah’s gift. The dedication ends in a feast of communion.

So the witnesses see Judah’s offering completed with peace offerings, the most numerous victims of all, and the whole gift summed. The witnesses note the abundance: “two oxen, five rams, five he goats, five lambs of the first year” — the peace offerings far outnumbered the single victims of the burnt and sin offerings. They mark the reason Wesley gives: the peace offerings were more numerous because, of the peace offering, the offerers themselves partook; the princes, the priests, and some of the people made a feast before the LORD out of them, eating together in fellowship and joy; so many beasts were needed for so great a company. They observe Gill’s summary that here were gathered all the sorts of offering — the meat (and drink) offering of flour and oil, the burnt offering of entire consecration, the sin offering of atonement, and the peace offering of communion; the dedication embraced the whole range of worship, every kind of approach to God. They note the fitness of ending in the peace offering: after consecration and atonement came communion, the shared feast of reconciliation and gladness, fellowship with God and with one another at his table. They observe the typology the witnesses carry: Christ is the peace offering, our peace (Ephesians 2:14), on whom the believer feasts in joyful communion. They note the closing words, “this was the offering of Nahshon,” setting his whole gift on record. They observe that Judah’s offering became the pattern for all that followed. The verse thus names the peace offerings completing Judah’s gift: the feast of communion — Issachar offering next in the verses following.
18:
On Numbers 7:18 — “On the second day Nethaneel the son of Zuar, prince of Issachar, did offer” — the witnesses mark Issachar offering on the second day.

The Reformed Gill: on the second day Nethaneel the son of Zuar, prince of Issachar, offered, as Judah had on the first.

The verse names the second offerer. Nethaneel, prince of Issachar, offers on the second day. The second tribe of the eastern camp gives in turn.

So the witnesses see Issachar, through its prince Nethaneel, offer on the second day, following Judah in the order of the camp. The witnesses note the succession: “on the second day Nethaneel the son of Zuar, prince of Issachar, did offer” — Gill observes that Issachar offered next after Judah, as in the camp Issachar pitched next to Judah on the east, under the same leading standard; the order of the dedication followed exactly the order of the encampment. They mark that each prince had his own appointed day, so that Issachar’s prince came forward on the second, neither before his time nor after; God’s arrangement gave every tribe its turn in order. They observe the equality the witnesses see in the repetition: Issachar brought the very same offering as Judah, in nothing less and in nothing more; though the tribes differed in number and in dignity, at the altar they stood alike, each presenting an identical gift, that none might be exalted above another in the worship of God. They note that Scripture sets down Issachar’s offering in full, though it was the same as Judah’s, not lumping the tribes together but honouring each by its own record; God takes particular notice of every tribe’s devotion. They observe that the second day continued the sustained feast of dedication, the joy prolonged tribe by tribe. They note that the line of the offering held: each prince in his day, each gift the same. The verse thus names the second offerer: Nethaneel of Issachar — his offering, like Judah’s, detailed in the next verses.

So the witnesses see, beyond the bare naming of the day and the prince, the steady order of the whole dedication: that God appointed each tribe its turn, and that the gifts followed the ranking of the camp rather than the rivalries of men. They observe that the recording of each prince by name, and the repetition of the same offering for each, is no idle redundancy but a witness that God values every tribe alike and takes particular notice of each one’s devotion; he will not lump them together, but writes out each in full. They note that the sustained dedication, prolonged day by day, made the joy of the people a continued feast, every tribe having its own day of honour before the LORD. They mark that so the church is ordered, each member and ministry given its place and time, none preferred, all serving alike the God who dwells in the midst.
19:
On Numbers 7:19 — “He offered for his offering one silver charger… one silver bowl… both of them full of fine flour mingled with oil for a meat offering” — the witnesses mark Issachar’s silver vessels and meat offering.

The Reformed Gill refers to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:13), Issachar’s being the same.

The verse repeats the silver vessels. Issachar brings the same charger, bowl, and meat offering as Judah. The gift is identical.

So the witnesses see Issachar bring the very same silver vessels and meat offering that Judah had brought. The witnesses note the sameness Gill marks by referring back to the first prince’s gift (Numbers 7:13): the silver charger of a hundred and thirty shekels, the silver bowl of seventy, both filled with fine flour mingled with oil for a meat offering — in every particular the same as Nahshon’s. They mark the meaning of the meat offering, here repeated: the fine flour and oil, the fruits of the ground offered to God, owned him as the giver of the people’s bread and dedicated their sustenance to him; the oil, as ever, betokening the grace that sanctifies. They observe the costliness of the silver vessels, weighed by the shekel of the sanctuary, a worthy gift from the prince on behalf of his tribe. They note the witnesses’ reflection on the identical gifts: that God required of each tribe the same offering, neither more from the great nor less from the small, so that all came alike to the altar; the equality of the offering taught the equality of the tribes before God in worship. They observe that Scripture, rather than abridging, records Issachar’s vessels in full, that the tribe’s devotion be honoured by its own naming; what is offered to God is not too small to be written. They note the typology held from Judah’s gift: the pure flour pointing to Christ the bread of life. The verse thus repeats the silver vessels and meat offering for Issachar — the golden spoon following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in this repeated gift of silver and meal a token of the whole chapter’s teaching. They observe that the costly vessels, weighed by the shekel of the sanctuary, and filled with the fine flour and oil of the meat offering, owned God as the giver of all the people’s substance, and dedicated their daily bread to him who supplied it. They note that the identical charger and bowl, brought by tribe after tribe, taught that all came alike to the altar, the great and the small, the freeborn and the handmaid’s son, none with a richer gift and none with a poorer. They mark that Scripture’s patient recording of each vessel in full honours each tribe’s devotion by name. They observe the typology held throughout: the pure flour without leaven points to Christ, the bread of life, perfect and undefiled, mingled with the oil of the sanctifying Spirit.
20:
On Numbers 7:20 — “One spoon of gold of ten shekels, full of incense” — the witnesses mark Issachar’s golden spoon of incense.

The Reformed Gill refers to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:14), Issachar’s being the same.

The verse repeats the gold spoon. Issachar brings the same golden spoon of incense as Judah. The gift is identical.

So the witnesses see Issachar bring the same golden spoon, full of incense, that Judah had offered. The witnesses note the sameness Gill marks by referring to the first prince’s gift (Numbers 7:14): a spoon or censer of gold, ten shekels in weight, filled with the holy incense. They mark that this vessel, like Judah’s, was of gold, the more precious metal fitting the incense, which was burned before the LORD in the holy place; the things nearest the divine service were of gold. They observe the meaning of the incense, here repeated: it is the emblem of prayer, the prayers of the saints rising to God as a sweet savour (Revelation 8:3), and of the intercession of Christ, by whose merit the people’s prayers are made acceptable; so each tribe, in giving its spoon of incense, offered with its gifts the sweet odour of prayer. They note the witnesses’ observation on the identical offerings: every prince brought the same golden spoon of incense, that the prayer and praise of every tribe might rise alike to God, none preferred before another in this sweet service. They observe that Scripture records each spoon in full, honouring each tribe’s devotion by its own naming, though all the gifts were the same. They note that the gift joined gold and incense, the costliest metal and the emblem of prayer. The verse thus repeats the gold spoon of incense for Issachar — the burnt offering following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in this golden spoon of incense, brought by every prince alike, the offering of prayer joined to the gifts of substance and sacrifice. They observe that the costliest metal, gold, fitly bore the emblem of prayer, for the things nearest the holy service of God were of gold; and that the sweet incense, burned before the LORD, figured the prayers of the saints ascending as a sweet savour (Revelation 8:3), made acceptable through the intercession of Christ. They note that every tribe brought the same spoon and the same incense, that the prayer and praise of all might rise alike to God, none preferred and none despised in this sweet service. They mark that Scripture records each spoon in full, writing out the devotion of every tribe by name, so that the prayers of all the people stand on record before God, who forgets none of his own.
21:
On Numbers 7:21 — “One young bullock, one ram, one lamb of the first year, for a burnt offering” — the witnesses mark Issachar’s burnt offering.

The Reformed Gill refers to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:15), Issachar’s being the same.

The verse repeats the burnt offering. Issachar brings the same bullock, ram, and lamb as Judah. The gift is identical.

So the witnesses see Issachar bring the same burnt offering — a bullock, a ram, and a lamb — that Judah had offered. They note that the dedication was sealed, tribe by tribe, with whole burnt sacrifice. The verse thus repeats the burnt offering for Issachar — the sin offering following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in this burnt offering, brought by every prince alike, the entire consecration of each tribe to God. They observe that the bullock, ram, and lamb were wholly consumed upon the altar, ascending in flame and smoke to the LORD, the offerer keeping no part; the whole burnt offering was the costliest token of devotion, the giving up of all to God. They note that each tribe brought the same three victims, neither more nor less, so that all were alike consecrated to him who dwelt in the midst, and no tribe could boast of a greater surrender or be charged with a smaller; in the worship of God there is no room for rivalry. 22:
On Numbers 7:22 — “One kid of the goats for a sin offering” — the witnesses mark Issachar’s sin offering.

The Reformed Gill refers to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:16), Issachar’s being the same.

The verse repeats the sin offering. Issachar brings the same kid for a sin offering as Judah. Atonement attends his thanksgiving too.

So the witnesses see Issachar, like Judah, join a sin offering to his joyful gifts. The witnesses note the sameness Gill marks by referring to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:16): one kid of the goats for a sin offering, brought with the burnt and peace offerings. They mark the truth the witnesses drew out at the first instance, here holding for every tribe alike: though the dedication offerings were by way of thanksgiving and joy, yet a sin offering was added to each, because even the best and gladdest services of God’s people are not free from sin and need atonement. They observe that not Judah only, but Issachar and every prince in turn, brought the kid of the sin offering; the confession of sin and the need of cleansing belonged to every tribe, none being so righteous as to come without atonement. They note the humbling and gracious lesson: that all Israel, in its leaders and its tribes, owned at the altar that its very thank offerings required the covering of an atoning sacrifice; the sin offering ran like a thread through all the joyful giving. They observe the typology held from Judah’s gift: the sin offering points to Christ, made sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21), through whom alone the worship of every tribe and soul is accepted. They note that joy and the confession of sin were rightly joined in every prince’s offering. The verse thus repeats the sin offering for Issachar — the peace offerings following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in this sin offering, brought by every prince alike, the confession that even the gladdest worship needs atonement. They observe that, though the dedication gifts were offered in thanksgiving and joy, a sin offering was added to each, because no service of God’s people, however costly or sincere, is so pure that it does not need the cleansing of an atoning sacrifice. They note that every tribe in turn, the firstborn and the youngest, the freeborn and the handmaid’s son, brought the same kid of the goats; the need of pardon belonged to all, none being so righteous as to come without atonement. They mark the humbling and gracious lesson: that all Israel owned at the altar that its very thank offerings required a covering for sin. They observe the typology: the sin offering points to Christ, made sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21), through whom alone the worship of every tribe and soul is accepted.
23:
On Numbers 7:23 — “And for a sacrifice of peace offerings, two oxen, five rams, five he goats, five lambs of the first year: this was the offering of Nethaneel the son of Zuar” — the witnesses mark the peace offerings completing Issachar’s gift.

The Reformed Gill refers to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:17), Issachar’s being the same.

The verse completes Issachar’s gift. The same peace offerings close his offering as Judah’s. The dedication of the second day ends in communion.

So the witnesses see Issachar’s offering completed, like Judah’s, with the abundant peace offerings, and the whole gift set on record. The witnesses note the sameness Gill marks by referring to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:17): two oxen, five rams, five he-goats, and five lambs of the first year, for a sacrifice of peace offerings — the same numerous victims that Judah had brought. They mark the meaning of the peace offering, here repeated: of it the offerers partook, the prince, the priests, and the people feasting before the LORD in fellowship and joy; so many beasts were given because so great a company shared the sacred meal. They observe that the dedication of each day ended, as the first, in the peace offering — communion following consecration and atonement; after the burnt offering of surrender and the sin offering of cleansing came the peace offering of fellowship, the shared feast of reconciliation with God. They note the closing words, “this was the offering of Nethaneel the son of Zuar,” setting his whole gift on record by name; Scripture honours each prince and tribe by writing out its offering in full, though it was the same as the rest. They observe the typology held from Judah’s gift: Christ is the peace offering, our peace (Ephesians 2:14), on whom the redeemed feast in joyful communion. They note that the second day’s dedication closed in gladness and fellowship. The verse thus completes Issachar’s gift with the peace offerings — Zebulun offering next in the verses following.

So the witnesses see in these peace offerings, the most numerous of all the victims, the feast of communion that crowned each day’s dedication. They observe that of the peace offering the offerers themselves partook, the prince, the priests, and the people eating together before the LORD in fellowship and joy; so many beasts were given because so great a company shared the sacred meal. They note that the order of the offerings was complete and fitting: first the burnt offering of entire consecration, then the sin offering of atonement, and last the peace offering of communion — surrender, cleansing, and fellowship in their right sequence. They mark that Scripture sets down each prince’s whole gift by name, honouring every tribe’s devotion in full. They observe the typology held throughout: Christ is the peace offering, our peace (Ephesians 2:14), on whom the redeemed feast in joyful communion, reconciled to God.
24:
On Numbers 7:24 — “On the third day Eliab the son of Helon, prince of the children of Zebulun, did offer” — the witnesses mark Zebulun offering on the third day.

The Reformed Gill: on the third day Eliab the son of Helon, prince of Zebulun, offered, as the two princes before him.

The verse names the third offerer. Eliab, prince of Zebulun, offers on the third day. The third tribe of the eastern camp gives in turn.

So the witnesses see Zebulun, through its prince Eliab, offer on the third day, completing the eastern camp’s turn at the dedication. The witnesses note the succession: “on the third day Eliab the son of Helon, prince of the children of Zebulun, did offer” — Gill observes that Zebulun offered third, as in the camp Zebulun was the third tribe under Judah’s standard on the east; the order of the dedication kept exactly to the order of the encampment, the three brother-tribes of Leah offering on the first three days. They mark that each prince came forward on his appointed day, neither sooner nor later; God’s arrangement gave every tribe its turn in due order, and the eastern camp, which pitched first and marched first, offered first. They observe the equality the witnesses keep noting: Zebulun brought the same offering as Judah and Issachar, in nothing differing; though the tribes varied in number, at the altar they stood alike, each presenting an identical gift, that none be exalted above another. They note that Scripture records Zebulun’s offering in full, as the others, not lumping the tribes together but honouring each by its own naming; God takes particular notice of every tribe’s devotion. They observe that the third day continued the sustained feast of dedication, the joy prolonged tribe by tribe through the eastern camp. They note that the order held: each prince on his day, each gift the same. The verse thus names the third offerer: Eliab of Zebulun — his offering detailed in the next verses.

So the witnesses see, beyond the bare naming of the day and the prince, the steady order of the whole dedication: that God appointed each tribe its turn, and that the gifts followed the ranking of the camp rather than the rivalries of men. They observe that the recording of each prince by name, and the repetition of the same offering for each, is no idle redundancy but a witness that God values every tribe alike and takes particular notice of each one’s devotion; he will not lump them together, but writes out each in full. They mark that so the church is ordered, each member and ministry given its place and time, none preferred, all serving alike the God who dwells in the midst.
25:

The Reformed Gill refers to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:13), Zebulun’s being the same.

The verse repeats the silver vessels. Zebulun brings the same charger, bowl, and meat offering as the princes before. The gift is identical.

So the witnesses see Zebulun bring the same silver vessels and meat offering as the princes before him. The witnesses note the sameness Gill marks by referring to the first prince’s gift (Numbers 7:13): the silver charger of a hundred and thirty shekels, the silver bowl of seventy, both filled with fine flour mingled with oil for a meat offering — in every particular the same as Nahshon’s and Nethaneel’s. 26:

The Reformed Gill refers to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:14), Zebulun’s being the same.

27:

They observe that, though the dedication gifts were offered in thanksgiving and joy, a sin offering was added to each, because no service of God’s people, however costly or sincere, is so pure that it does not need the cleansing of an atoning sacrifice. They note that every tribe in turn, the firstborn and the youngest, the freeborn and the handmaid’s son, brought the same kid of the goats; the need of pardon belonged to all, none being so righteous as to come without atonement. They mark the humbling and gracious lesson: that all Israel owned at the altar that its very thank offerings required a covering for sin. They observe the typology: the sin offering points to Christ, made sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21), through whom alone the worship of every tribe and soul is accepted.
41:
On Numbers 7:41 — “And for a sacrifice of peace offerings, two oxen, five rams, five he goats, five lambs of the first year: this was the offering of Shelumiel the son of Zurishaddai” — the witnesses mark the peace offerings completing Simeon’s gift.

The Reformed Gill refers to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:17), Simeon’s being the same.

The verse completes Simeon’s gift. The same peace offerings close his offering. The fifth day ends in communion.

So the witnesses see Simeon’s offering completed with the peace offerings, the fifth day’s dedication ending in the feast of fellowship. The witnesses note the sameness Gill marks by referring to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:17): two oxen, five rams, five he-goats, and five lambs of the first year, for peace offerings — the same numerous victims the princes before him had brought. They mark the meaning of the peace offering, here repeated: of it the offerers partook, the prince, the priests, and the people feasting before the LORD in fellowship and joy; so many beasts were given because so great a company shared the sacred meal. They observe that each day’s dedication ended in the peace offering — communion following consecration and atonement; after the burnt offering of surrender and the sin offering of cleansing came the peace offering of fellowship, the shared feast of reconciliation with God. They note the closing words, “this was the offering of Shelumiel the son of Zurishaddai,” setting his whole gift on record by name; Scripture honours each prince and tribe by writing out its offering in full, though it was the same as the rest. They observe the typology held throughout: Christ the peace offering, our peace (Ephesians 2:14), on whom the redeemed feast in joyful communion. They note that the fifth day closed in gladness and fellowship. The verse thus completes Simeon’s gift with the peace offerings — Gad offering next in the verses following.

So the witnesses see in these peace offerings, the most numerous of all the victims, the feast of communion that crowned each day’s dedication. They observe that of the peace offering the offerers themselves partook, the prince, the priests, and the people eating together before the LORD in fellowship and joy; so many beasts were given because so great a company shared the sacred meal. They note that the order of the offerings was complete and fitting: first the burnt offering of entire consecration, then the sin offering of atonement, and last the peace offering of communion — surrender, cleansing, and fellowship in their right sequence. They mark that Scripture sets down each prince’s whole gift by name, honouring every tribe’s devotion in full. They observe the typology held throughout: Christ is the peace offering, our peace (Ephesians 2:14), on whom the redeemed feast in joyful communion, reconciled to God.
42:
On Numbers 7:42 — “On the sixth day Eliasaph the son of Deuel, prince of the children of Gad, offered” — the witnesses mark Gad offering on the sixth day.

The Reformed Gill: on the sixth day Eliasaph the son of Deuel, prince of Gad, offered, the third tribe of the southern camp.

The verse names the sixth offerer. Eliasaph, prince of Gad, offers on the sixth day. The third tribe of the southern camp gives in turn.

So the witnesses see Gad, through its prince Eliasaph, offer on the sixth day, completing the southern camp’s turn at the dedication. The witnesses note the succession: “on the sixth day Eliasaph the son of Deuel, prince of the children of Gad, offered” — Gill observes that Gad offered sixth, as in the camp Gad was the third tribe under Reuben’s standard on the south; the order of the dedication followed exactly the order of the encampment, the southern camp’s three tribes offering on the fourth, fifth, and sixth days. They mark that each prince came forward on his appointed day, neither sooner nor later; God’s arrangement gave every tribe its turn in due order. They observe the equality the witnesses keep noting: Gad brought the same offering as the princes before him, in nothing differing; though Gad was the son of a handmaid, at the altar it stood alike with the sons of Leah and Rachel, presenting an identical gift, that none be exalted or abased above another in the worship of God. They note that Scripture records Gad’s offering in full, as the others, honouring the tribe by its own naming; God takes particular notice of every tribe’s devotion, the handmaid’s son no less than the freeborn. They observe that with Gad the southern camp’s three tribes had all offered, half the twelve days complete. They note that the order held: each prince on his day, each gift the same. The verse thus names the sixth offerer: Eliasaph of Gad — his offering detailed in the next verses.

So the witnesses see, beyond the bare naming of the day and the prince, the steady order of the whole dedication: that God appointed each tribe its turn, and that the gifts followed the ranking of the camp rather than the rivalries of men. They observe that the recording of each prince by name, and the repetition of the same offering for each, is no idle redundancy but a witness that God values every tribe alike and takes particular notice of each one’s devotion; he will not lump them together, but writes out each in full. They note that the sustained dedication, prolonged day by day, made the joy of the people a continued feast, every tribe having its own day of honour before the LORD. They mark that so the church is ordered, each member and ministry given its place and time, none preferred, all serving alike the God who dwells in the midst.
43:
On Numbers 7:43 — “His offering was one silver charger… one silver bowl… both full of fine flour mingled with oil for a meat offering” — the witnesses mark Gad’s silver vessels and meat offering.

The Reformed Gill refers to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:13), Gad’s being the same.

The verse repeats the silver vessels. Gad brings the same charger, bowl, and meat offering. The gift is identical.

So the witnesses see Gad bring the same silver vessels and meat offering as the princes before him. The witnesses note the sameness Gill marks by referring to the first prince’s gift (Numbers 7:13): the silver charger of a hundred and thirty shekels, the silver bowl of seventy, both filled with fine flour mingled with oil for a meat offering — in every particular the same as the princes before. They mark the meaning of the meat offering, here repeated: the fine flour and oil, the fruits of the ground offered to God, owned him as the giver of the people’s bread and consecrated their sustenance to him; the oil betokening the sanctifying grace. They observe the costliness of the silver vessels, weighed by the shekel of the sanctuary, a worthy gift from the prince on behalf of his tribe. They note the witnesses’ reflection on the identical gifts running through the chapter: that God required the same offering of each tribe, neither more from one nor less from another, so that all came alike to the altar; the equality of the gift taught the equality of the tribes before God in worship, the handmaid’s son giving as the freeborn. They observe that Scripture records Gad’s vessels in full, that his tribe’s devotion be honoured by its own naming, though the gift was the same. They note the typology held throughout: the pure flour pointing to Christ the bread of life. The verse thus repeats the silver vessels and meat offering for Gad — the golden spoon following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in this repeated gift of silver and meal a token of the whole chapter’s teaching. They observe that the costly vessels, weighed by the shekel of the sanctuary, and filled with the fine flour and oil of the meat offering, owned God as the giver of all the people’s substance, and dedicated their daily bread to him who supplied it. They note that the identical charger and bowl, brought by tribe after tribe, taught that all came alike to the altar, the great and the small, the freeborn and the handmaid’s son, none with a richer gift and none with a poorer. They mark that Scripture’s patient recording of each vessel in full honours each tribe’s devotion by name. They observe the typology held throughout: the pure flour without leaven points to Christ, the bread of life, perfect and undefiled, mingled with the oil of the sanctifying Spirit.
44:
On Numbers 7:44 — “One golden spoon of ten shekels, full of incense” — the witnesses mark Gad’s golden spoon of incense.

The Reformed Gill refers to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:14), Gad’s being the same.

The verse repeats the gold spoon. Gad brings the same golden spoon of incense. The gift is identical.

So the witnesses see Gad bring the same golden spoon, full of incense, as the princes before him. The witnesses note the sameness Gill marks by referring to the first prince’s gift (Numbers 7:14): a spoon or censer of gold, ten shekels in weight, filled with the holy incense. They mark that this vessel, like the others, was of gold, the more precious metal fitting the incense burned before the LORD; the things nearest the holy service were of gold. They observe the meaning of the incense, here repeated: it is the emblem of prayer, the prayers of the saints ascending to God as a sweet savour (Revelation 8:3), and of the intercession of Christ, by whose merit the people’s prayers are made acceptable; so Gad, with its gifts, offered the sweet odour of prayer. They note the witnesses’ observation on the identical spoons: every prince brought the same golden spoon of incense, that the prayer and praise of every tribe might rise alike to God, none preferred before another, the handmaid’s son’s prayer as acceptable as the freeborn’s. They observe that Scripture records each spoon in full, honouring each tribe’s devotion by its own naming, though all the gifts were the same. They note that gold and incense were joined in the gift, the costliest metal bearing the emblem of prayer. The verse thus repeats the gold spoon of incense for Gad — the burnt offering following in the next verse.

So the witnesses see in this golden spoon of incense, brought by every prince alike, the offering of prayer joined to the gifts of substance and sacrifice. They observe that the costliest metal, gold, fitly bore the emblem of prayer, for the things nearest the holy service of God were of gold; and that the sweet incense, burned before the LORD, figured the prayers of the saints ascending as a sweet savour (Revelation 8:3), made acceptable through the intercession of Christ. They note that every tribe brought the same spoon and the same incense, that the prayer and praise of all might rise alike to God, none preferred and none despised in this sweet service. They mark that Scripture records each spoon in full, writing out the devotion of every tribe by name, so that the prayers of all the people stand on record before God, who forgets none of his own.
45:
On Numbers 7:45 — “One young bullock, one ram, one lamb of the first year, for a burnt offering” — the witnesses mark Gad’s burnt offering.

The Reformed Gill refers to the first prince’s offering (Numbers 7:15), Gad’s being the same.

The verse repeats the burnt offering. Gad brings the same bullock, ram, and lamb. The gift is identical.

So the witnesses see Gad bring the same burnt offering — a bullock, a ram, and a lamb — as the princes before him. The witnesses note the sameness Gill marks by referring to the first prince’s gift (Numbers 7:15): the three victims brought together and wholly offered to God upon the altar. They mark the nature of the burnt offering, here repeated: it was consumed entire in the flame, ascending wholly to God, the offerer keeping no part; it expressed the total consecration of the tribe and all it had to the LORD. They observe the fitness of each tribe bringing the same whole burnt offering at the dedication: every tribe alike surrendered its best to God, none holding back more than another; the equal offering taught that all the tribes were equally consecrated to him who dwelt in the midst. They note the witnesses’ reflection on the unbroken repetition through the chapter: God required and Scripture records the same burnt offering from each, that no tribe might claim to have given more, and none be thought to have given less; in the worship of God there is no room for rivalry, but all draw near alike, the handmaid’s son with the freeborn. They observe the typology held from Judah’s gift: the unblemished victims wholly offered point to Christ, who offered himself without spot to God (Hebrews 9:14). They note that the dedication continued, day by day, sealed with whole burnt sacrifice. The verse thus repeats the burnt offering for Gad — his sin and peace offerings continuing beyond this portion.

So the witnesses see in this burnt offering, brought by every prince alike, the entire consecration of each tribe to God. They observe that the bullock, ram, and lamb were wholly consumed upon the altar, ascending in flame and smoke to the LORD, the offerer keeping no part; the whole burnt offering was the costliest token of devotion, the giving up of all to God. They note that each tribe brought the same three victims, neither more nor less, so that all were alike consecrated to him who dwelt in the midst, and no tribe could boast of a greater surrender or be charged with a smaller; in the worship of God there is no room for rivalry. They mark the typology that runs through the chapter: the unblemished victims, wholly offered, point to Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God (Hebrews 9:14), the perfect burnt offering of entire devotion.
46:

JFB and Gill note that "one kid of the goats for a sin offering" — the prince of Gad (Eliasaph the son of Deuel, on the sixth day) brought, with the rest of his offering, one kid of the goats for a sin offering; the one kid of the goats (the sin offering, the goat that bore the sin, Leviticus 4:23); the for a sin offering (the atonement for sin); the figure (the sin offering brought even in the joyful dedication, the acknowledgment that even the willing offerer is a sinner needing atonement; the kid of the goats a figure of Christ made sin for us, "he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin," 2 Corinthians 5:21; the truth that all our offerings must rest on the atonement, the sin offering at the foundation of the dedication; the gift accepted only through the blood that covers sin).

The verse displays the sin offering of the prince of Gad.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the offering — "one kid of the goats for a sin offering," the sin offering (JFB, Gill); "one kid of the goats," the goat (Leviticus 4:23, Gill); "for a sin offering," the atonement (2 Corinthians 5:21, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sin offering. The verse marks the kid: the sin offering. The verse displays the atonement: for sin. The verse displays the offerer: a sinner. The verse displays the peace offerings of Gad (v.47).
