On "this was the offering of Eliasaph the son of Deuel," the grammatical-historical note the prince: "this was the offering of Eliasaph the son of Deuel" — the prince named and his gift recorded (the devotion noted); the figure (the peace offering, communion through Christ, Ephesians 2:14); the providence (Gad's offering complete, Ephraim's to follow, vv.48). The verse displays the peace offerings of the prince of Gad.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witness agrees: the offering — "for a sacrifice of peace offerings, two oxen, five rams, five he goats, five lambs of the first year: this was the offering of Eliasaph the son of Deuel," the peace offerings (Gill); "for a sacrifice of peace offerings, two oxen, five rams, five he goats, five lambs of the first year," the peace offerings (Leviticus 3, Gill); "this was the offering of Eliasaph the son of Deuel," the prince (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the peace offerings. The verse marks the peace offerings: abundant. The verse displays the fellowship: with God. The verse displays the prince: named. The verse displays the communion: in Christ. The peace offerings display the fellowship with God through the atonement. The verse displays the offering: of fellowship. The verse displays the prince: Eliasaph. The verse displays the peace: in Christ. The peace offerings of the prince of Gad display the communion with God: this was the offering of Eliasaph the son of Deuel (Leviticus 3; Ephesians 2:14; Colossians 1:20) — the peace offerings of fellowship that crown the gift, a figure of the communion with God through Christ our peace, the prince's whole offering recorded by name. The verse displays the offering of the prince of Ephraim (vv.48-53).
48:
"On the seventh day Elishama the son of Ammihud, prince of the children of Ephraim, offered:" The witnesses note the offering of the prince of Ephraim, each prince in his day bringing the same gift — the crux of the equal and willing devotion of the tribes. On "On the seventh day Elishama the son of Ammihud, prince of the children of Ephraim, offered," interpreters expound the ordered, equal offerings. The reformed and the dominant reading note the pattern — "on the seventh day Elishama... prince of Ephraim, offered": each of the twelve princes, on his own appointed day, brought an offering identical to all the rest, and each was recorded separately by name; the grammatical-historical Clarke and witnesses note the significance — the equality and order of the offerings (each prince in his turn, one each day, every tribe bringing exactly the same, none preferred above another); the lesson — the equal honor and devotion of all the tribes (the same charger, bowl, spoon, bullock, ram, lamb, kid, and peace offerings from every prince, the equal standing of all before God, no tribe greater in the offering than another); the wonder that the Spirit records each prince's identical offering at full length, twelve times over, not abridging them — God's particular notice of each, every tribe's gift set down in full, none slighted or merged with another, "the Lord taketh pleasure in his people," Psalm 149:4; the lesson that God values each offerer and each gift, however alike, and records each by name, as the names of the redeemed are written; the figure — the willing and equal devotion of God's people, each bringing his offering in his order, "every man according as he purposeth in his heart... for God loveth a cheerful giver," 2 Corinthians 9:7; the willing-hearted offering for God's house, Exodus 35:21, 29; and the order that does all things "decently and in order," 1 Corinthians 14:40; the figure of the church where each member has his place and his gift, equally valued, each recorded and accepted of God.

On "on the seventh day Elishama the son of Ammihud," the witnesses note the day and prince. Gill and Clarke note Elishama of Ephraim offering on the seventh day (each prince in his turn, the ordered offering) — the order (each in his day).

On "prince of the children of Ephraim, offered," interpreters note the equal gift. Gill notes the prince of Ephraim bringing the same offering as the rest (the equal devotion, Exodus 35:29) — the figure (the equal honor of the tribes, 2 Corinthians 9:7); the providence (each prince recorded, the offering detailed, vv.49-53). The verse displays the offering of the prince of Ephraim.

The witnesses agree the substance: each prince, on his own day, brought an identical offering, recorded separately by name (Clarke, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the equal and willing devotion of the tribes: the ordered offerings — each prince in his turn bringing exactly the same gift, every tribe equally honored, each recorded at full length by name (God's particular notice of each, Psalm 149:4), a figure of the willing and equal devotion of God's people (2 Corinthians 9:7; Exodus 35:29) and of the order of the church where each member's gift is valued and accepted (1 Corinthians 14:40). The verse displays the equal-devotion crux. The offering of the prince of Ephraim displays the crux: on the seventh day Elishama... prince of Ephraim, offered (Psalm 149:4; 2 Corinthians 9:7; Exodus 35:29; 1 Corinthians 14:40) — each prince in his day bringing the same willing offering, every tribe equally honored and recorded by name, a figure of the equal and willing devotion of God's people. The verse displays the silver charger and bowl of Ephraim (v.49).
49:

The verse displays the silver charger and bowl of the prince of Ephraim.

The verse displays the golden spoon of incense of Ephraim (the next verse).
50:
